entry.title: Warehouse Order Confirmation & PGI API
entry.content:
Dear SAP Experts,

  We have successfully integrated SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud Embedded EWM with an external system and are able to create Warehouse Tasks (WT) in the background using the API API_WAREHOUSE_ORDER_TASK_2, which is working as expected. However, we require further guidance and the correct APIs to complete the subsequent steps in the process, specifically to confirm the Warehouse Order (WO) or related Warehouse Tasks in the background and then post the Goods Issue (PGI) automatically via API, so that the outbound delivery process can be fully completed without manual intervention.

Currently, the WT creation is successful, but we need the appropriate API names, endpoints, and payload structure for (1) Warehouse Order / Warehouse Task confirmation and (2) Goods Issue posting, along with any prerequisite configuration required in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud to ensure seamless end-to-end automation of the warehouse execution process. 

Kindly provide the API'S warehouse order confirmation & through warehouse monitor Fiori App using do the Goods Issue Posting.


------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: Warehouse Order Confirmation & PGI API
entry.content:
For SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition – Embedded EWM, the APIs and documentation for warehouse execution and outbound delivery processing are available in the official SAP Help Portal and SAP API Business Hub.

1. Warehouse Task / Warehouse Order APIs

The API used to create, read and confirm warehouse tasks is:

  • Warehouse Order and Task (A2X) API

Official documentation:

Operations for Warehouse Order and Task API

This API provides operations for:

  • Creating Warehouse Tasks

  • Reading Warehouse Orders and Tasks

  • Confirming Warehouse Tasks

  • Cancelling Warehouse Tasks

As described in the documentation, Warehouse Orders themselves are not confirmed directly via API. The Warehouse Order is automatically completed when all related Warehouse Tasks are confirmed.

 

2. Goods Issue Posting for Outbound Delivery

After the picking warehouse tasks are confirmed, the Goods Issue must be posted on the Outbound Delivery.

The supported API is:

  • Outbound Delivery (A2X) API

Official documentation:

Operations for Warehouse Outbound Delivery Order API

This API supports the Post Goods Issue action for outbound deliveries once the warehouse execution steps are completed.

Any follow up question or further clarification, we remain at your service here!

 

Best regards,

SAP Support

entry.title: ‌🇫🇷‌ French SAP User Group Meeting
entry.content:
The French SAP User Group invites SAP customers and partners to a remote meeting dedicated to recent and upcoming developments in Human Capital Management France. This session provides a structured update on the product roadmap, regulatory topics, and operational feedback, with a strong focus on payroll and DSN-related changes.

The meeting is intended for SAP professionals involved in HCM France who are looking to stay informed about current decisions from French institutions, ongoing transitions, and practical lessons learned.

Agenda

  1. Presentation of development plan HCM France for the last 6 months and the next 6 months by PO HCM France
  2. “Fait générateur” (use contribution rates and thresholds of the for-period for active employees) : Information on the latest decisions from the Institutions for a productive transition in 2027
  3. DSN transition to P26V01 : Incident Review
  4. DSN Reminder and Substitution
  5. Update of Work Zone HCM France
  6. Q/A 
This meeting offers the opportunity to gain a consolidated view of recent developments, better understand upcoming changes, and exchange directly on open questions with SAP and the user community.

The event will be conducted in French.

REGISTER
 
Want to connect with others and learn more about HCM in France? Join the France - Human Capital Management Localization Work Zone group, where SAP experts share updates and guidance.

entry.title: SAP S/4HANA 2025 and FPS01: What’s New and Why It Matters
entry.content:
SAP S/4HANA 2025 continues SAP’s strategy of delivering innovation with stability. The release introduces targeted functional enhancements across cross‑application components and core business areas, while preserving compatibility and minimizing disruption for on‑premise and private cloud customers. SAP’s structured “What’s New” approach enables organizations to clearly identify relevant changes and assess business value efficiently.

The first Feature Package Stack (FPS01) builds on the initial release and enhances areas such as analytics, master data governance, value chain monitoring or asset management The update is designed for low adoption risk and requires significantly less effort than a full release upgrade. The release also expands intelligent capabilities, including Joule-enabled scenarios, embedding AI-driven insights and automation where they deliver concrete business outcomes and align with enterprise governance.

Overall, SAP S/4HANA 2025 and FPS01 offer a mature, stable innovation path—providing transparency, incremental improvements, and confidence for both upgrade planning and longterm roadmaps. The message is clear: progress without disruption.

Want to learn more, follow the link:

Whats new viewer

entry.title: What is Agentic Memory?
entry.content:
Agentic memory is a hot topic in the AI industry: big, B2C services like ChatGPT now have memory capabilities, popular agent development frameworks like langgraph define interfaces for memory interactions, and out-of-the-box memory solutions like mem0 provide plug-and-play memory capabilities to AI agents.
 
Why is that? From a 10,000-foot perspective, we can quickly state that agentic memory is about agents "remembering" past interactions to perform better in the future. Without memory, AI systems suffer amnesia, starting blank with every interaction.

But when exactly do agentic systems need memory? What are the business use (and do not use) cases? How does agentic memory differ from other types of memory, and how can we define it? This is what this article is about.
 

When to Use Agentic Memory?

To better reason about when to use agentic memory, I've found it helpful to start from first principles. My initial approach is to examine the types of information that should and should not be stored in agentic memory. The following considerations act as a thought experiment: a strong case for agentic memory begins to emerge when the information to be stored within agentic memory meets the following criteria and avoids the listed counterexamples:
 
 
First Principles Perspective: What to store in agentic memory and what not.
The information is...
Counterexample
Valuable across multiple interactions, carrying significance beyond a single session.A one-off query for a customer's order status. This fact should be retrieved via a live API call.
Novel, meaning it is not already contained within or contradicted by the base LLM's pre-trained knowledge.A request for the definition of "SWOT analysis." This is standard business knowledge already known by the model, making memory storage redundant.
Emergent and unspecified, meaning it is unknown at design time and is only discovered through interaction.A company's official list of approved software vendors. This is pre-defined information, best handled by a dedicated retrieval (RAG) system or prompting.
Unavailable from external systems like databases or APIs that could be queried directly.A customer's billing address. This structured, sensitive data must live in and be queried from a secure CRM, not duplicated in the agent's memory.
Constructed by the agent from its inputs, feedback, and self-reflection.None, this is a prerequisite. If this cannot be achieved, memory cannot be built.
 
 
 
Conversely, if any of these conditions are not met, we should consider whether a more conventional tool, such as a direct database query or a standard RAG system, would be a better solution.
In contrast, the following scenarios are prime examples of situations in which agentic memory is not just helpful but often necessary for an agent to perform its tasks effectively.
 
 
Use Cases for Agentic Memory
Use CaseDescriptionExample
PersonalizationTailor the agent's workflow, communication style, or outputs to the needs, preferences, and history of a specific user.A customer service agent remembers a user's technical expertise level and provides advanced troubleshooting steps instead of basic ones.
Agile ContextualizationProvide the agent with narrow-scope, limited-lifespan information to be used across multiple interactions within a specific project, case, or workflow.An agent helping a team with a two-week software remembers conversations, decisions, and code snippets related only to that sprint.
Extended Working MemoryProvide an agent with relevant facts from a long history of interactions and tool use, overcoming the fixed-size limitations of an LLM's context window.During a long-running legal case analysis, the agent pulls key precedents from conversations held months ago, even if they exceed the context window.
Multi-Agent OrchestrationServe as a shared knowledge exchange layer, or "blackboard," for multiple agents to coordinate their work on a mutual goal.In a research team, one agent finds a key statistic and saves it to shared memory, allowing another agent to incorporate it into the final report immediately.
Curation of Latent KnowledgeExtract and make persistent the undocumented "tribal knowledge" of experts on business procedures or semantics from their interactions with the agent.By working with a senior engineer through a system diagnostics case, a support agent learns an undocumented process and saves it for future use.
Agentic Self-ImprovementAllow an agent to reflect on its own performance and environmental feedback to refine its approach for future tasks systematically.After a user marks a summary as "unhelpful," the agent analyzes the feedback and adjusts its summarization strategy to be more detailed next time.
 
 
 
These use cases — from personalization to self-improvement — suggest that agentic memory behaves more like human memory than computer databases. Let’s explore that distinction to arrive at a clear definition.

 

Trying to Define "Agentic Memory"

To delineate Agentic Memory further, I find it useful to examine its relationship to the concepts of Computer Memory and Human Memory. Here is a glimpse at my current point of view on these terms and their characteristics:
 
Computer Memory is often described and discussed through the technical lens of read-write speeds, size, volatility, scalability, and cost. These are also the dimensions used to cluster and characterize types of computer memory (e.g., cache vs. RAM=memory vs. secondary storage, vs. cloud storage). In computer science, the term is driven by "how" to store things. "What" to do with the memory is often discussed within the context of files, file systems, and databases. In computer science, memory is closely tied to the underlying substrate.
 
Based on my intuitive understanding (not a neuroscientist!) of Human Memory, I tend to discuss it from the perspective of "what" or through the lens of its lifecycle phases and big-picture purpose ("why"). Terms like certainty, connection, validity (e.g., time, scope), importance, attention, meaning, or interpretation come to mind. The lines between different types of memory, such as long-term and short-term, are less distinct than in computer memory. I also strongly associate human memory with learning mechanisms like self-reflection. E.g., humans start to actively search for confirmation of memories after a certain amount of time, and they constantly reconnect and restructure memories. Thus, my understanding of human memory also assumes a natural connection to learning.
 
I know of no singular, widely accepted definition for Agentic Memory. Defining it is a matter of perspective and emphasis. From my point of view, I find it helpful to employ a working definition closer to my perception of human memory, focusing on "why", "what", and lifecycle aspects instead of the memory substrate:
 
 
 
Agentic Memory is a system designed to manage agent-specific, emergent information that holds persistent value. It provides this service to an AI agent by managing the complete memory lifecycle:
  • Formation – Capturing novel insights from interactions and experiences.
  • Evolution – Refining, synthesizing, and connecting stored knowledge through reflection.
  • Retrieval – Intelligently surfacing relevant information to enhance the agent's context and decision-making for future tasks.
 
 
 
Memory evolution may not just involve memory updates but also strategic consolidation, optimization, rephrasing, interpretation, and hierarchization of memories aimed at improving agentic performance. In contrast to memory formation and retrieval, I consider it optional.
 
But even without memory evolution, I do not see agentic memory without learning! Instead, the very essence of engineering agentic memory systems is to define the principles and guidelines that govern the formation (and evolution) of memories and to provide a suitable substrate for storing and retrieving the anticipated information.
 

What's Next?

In my next blog post, I will share a summary of core agentic memory concepts, including a deep dive into memory lifecycle phases, an overview of different memory types, and memory architectures. Stay tuned and feel free to share your thoughts on this point of view. 
 
 
# CTO Research and Innovation Munich
@ladenaw

entry.title: SAP 2026: Between standard power and individual competitive advantage
entry.content:
The world of SAP SE is changing. While SAP stock and the SAP dividend dominate the headlines of the financial press, companies are facing a much deeper question: How much standardization is necessary and how much individuality is permissible?

At Toraman Consulting, we follow a clear principle: The SAP standard is the foundation, but we don't condemn in-house developments . It's about maintaining a balance.

The foundation: Why the standard is more important today than ever

With the rise of SAP Cloud and innovations like Joule (SAP's new AI assistant), a clean core is becoming essential. Those who are too deeply entrenched in the system code of the old SAP R/3 world cannot fully leverage the advantages of S/4HANA and the new Joule agents . The standard ensures stability, update capability, and access to state-of-the-art functions in modules such as SAP MM , SAP SD , and SAP PP .

The optional part: Where in-house developments make the difference

But we also know that a company isn't successful simply by doing the exact same thing as everyone else. Real competitive advantages often lie in processes that are anything but run-of-the-mill.

Our philosophy at Toraman Consulting :

  • Weighing options instead of rejecting: We carefully examine where the standard is sufficient and where a targeted extension provides the decisive added value.
  • Securing advantages: If an in-house development strengthens your market position or makes your customers happier, then we will defend this solution.
  • Modern Development: We use the SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) to create individuality without contaminating the digital core.

Partnerships and Market Trends 2026

The market is responding to this need for expert knowledge. Large consulting firms like KPMG and Deloitte are heavily investing in strategic alliances to bridge the gap between standard software and customized AI solutions. As a partner in the SAP Open Ecosystem, we offer you precisely this flexibility: the security of a global market leader combined with the personalized support your business requires.

Conclusion: Balance wins

Whether you're building your SAP systems from scratch or need a complex migration strategy, let's discuss your options together. The goal is a system that's stable enough for the future, yet flexible enough to reflect your unique strengths.

entry.title: Update the floating license software for PowerDesigner 16.7
entry.content:
Hello,

We have PowerDesigner 16.7 with floating license management.
The license management executable is on a dedicated machine that needs to be migrated (Windows Server 2016 to 2022).
As part of this process, we would also like to update the licensing software, which appears to be FlexNet Licensing (v11.14.0.0 build 183228 x64_n6).

What would be the procedure to follow?


------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: Update the floating license software for PowerDesigne...
entry.content:
Hello,

PowerDesigner 16.7 with floating-type licenses indeed requires the installation of SySAM 2.4 or preferably 2.4 SP02.
KBA 3583186 specifies the SySAM upgrade requirement due to FlexNet Publisher 11.16.2 reaching end of life on December 20, 2024, and notes SySAM 2.4 SP02 uses FlexNet v11.16.2.0 build 242433

The SySAM 2.4 SP02 requires the Visual C++ Redistributable Package for Visual Studio 2013.
Since this redistributable reached end-of-support on April 9, 2024, SySAM 2.5.0 was released and is now included with PowerDesigner 16.7 SP09.

To download the software, you need to follow the steps listed below:
1. Connect to SAP Software Downloads
https://me.sap.com/softwarecenter/search/powerdesigner
2. Select 'SAP POWERDESIGNER 16.7 - Maintenance Product'.
3. Select 'SAP POWERDESIGNER 16.7'.
4. The screen is refreshed.
Click POWDES167009_0-80005353.ZIP to download the software PowerDesigner 16.7 SP09.
The file contains PowerDesigner_16.7.SP09.8174.exe, PowerDesigner_16.7.SP09.8174_DVD.zip, PowerDesigner_16.7.SP09.8174_Portal.16915.exe, PowerDesigner_16.7.SP09.8174_Proxy.exe, SQLAnywhere 17.0 SP1 PL19 For PowerDesigner 16.7 SP09.zip and SYSAM 2.5.0.zip.

Kind regards

entry.title: RFP Automation with Agentic AI: My Journey from Manual RFPs to Smarter Sourcing
entry.content:
How RFP automation works with SAP’s Sourcing Event Agent  

Keeping up with AI can begin to feel like a part‑time job on top of the one you already have. That’s why the real wins don’t come from chasing the latest model. They come when AI, people, processes, and culture evolve together-and when RFP automation removes grunt work so teams can focus on strategy. Nowhere is that more obvious than in strategic sourcing, where agentic AI is reshaping how we create RFPs, evaluate suppliers, and make decisions that matter. 

A trade show RFP that had me floundering 

Early in my career I led a marketing events team and was asked to produce an RFP for a set of trade show booths and the services that go with them. I’d responded to RFPs before, but I’d never written one. I had no idea what I was in for. I had to research vendors, define requirements, write each question so it could be universally understood, and then reconcile spreadsheet after spreadsheet to score responses. I spent nights trying to make sense of conflicting line items like installation fees that were listed throughout the responses in different places, ambiguous add‑ons, and late vendor clarifications that required another round of follow‑up. I learned in real time what not to do.  

That project taught me three things:

  1. Sourcing events are equal parts detail work and judgment. 
  2. Small omissions, like not asking whether a vendor’s “standard setup” included freight or on‑site labor, can become expensive surprises. 
  3. Much of the manual work added almost no strategic value. I found myself drowning in a process that didn’t support the real decision points.

RFP automation with agentic AI: Why the Sourcing Event Agent has me leaning in 

If I had to create that same RFP today, it would look completely different. Within SAP Ariba Sourcing, I can just tell the Sourcing Event Agent what I need in plain, everyday language, and it will do in minutes the research that once took days. It recommends vendors based on supplier history and trusted market benchmarks, generates standardized RFP questions, and assembles a clean, comparison-based vendor matrix. 

  • Top agentic capabilities  
    • Vendor discovery that uses your supplier history and market data
    • Standardized question generation to improve comparability
    • Bid analysis that flags vague claims and missing compliance answers 
    • Easy tracing of sources for AI-assisted recommendations 
  • Estimated ROI* 
    • ~70% faster sourcing event launches 
    • ~80% faster bid analysis  

Because the Sourcing Event Agent connects to your qualified suppliers and past sourcing events, it drafts RFPs that reflect your environment and recent history. For regulated industries, the agent makes it simple to trace sources for recommendations.  

Moreover, the agent surfaces questions I wouldn’t necessarily think to ask. For example, it will prompt contingency questions such as, “What are your procedures for weather‑related delays?” and “What exactly is included in the standard setup and what triggers extra fees?” Those prompts could have helped my team avoid costly, last-minute surprises. 

Before vs. now 

Before: Now: 
A 100‑page RFP that required days of spreadsheet reconciliation and overtime to pull insights for stakeholders, while trying to hide from my leadership team how messy everything was.  A focused 30- to 40-page RFP generated in hours, with an automatically produced shortlist, and a vendor matrix that lets reviewers compare line items at a glance. 

See Sourcing Event Agent in action 

Speed isn't the only advantage. With AI embedded in the workflow, I can seamlessly access sourcing, supplier, and other data to understand the business context of different decisions. I don’t have to export files into a separate tool and hope for the best. The agent drafts RFPs with my business rules and sourcing history in mind. 

How RFP automation with agentic AI works  

Agentic AI needs the same briefing you’d give a colleague, including insights on the project objective and success criteria, scope and deliverables, timeline and key dates, budget and evaluation criteria, and any compliance constraints. Historical artifacts like previous RFPs, past responses, supplier performance data, and your scoring rubric help align outputs with your team’s needs. 

The amount of autonomy you give AI depends on your comfort level with risk. Risk-tolerant teams may allow the agent to draft full RFP sections and recommend shortlists, while accepting occasional oversights in exchange for speed. Risk-averse teams treat the agent like a smart assistant and review every output before it goes live. 

Most organizations find a middle ground and let the AI handle vendor discovery, market analysis, and initial drafts, while humans make final vendor selections, set evaluation criteria, and handle nuanced trade‑offs. The key is to be really honest when it comes to your organization’s DNA. Do you like to move fast and iterate, or do you prefer to go more slowly and feel certain everything's right the first time? 

Laying the foundation for agentic AI 

Agentic AI doesn't replace human judgment; it amplifies it. By removing repetitive tasks with tools like the Sourcing Event Agent, your procurement team can spend more time aligning strategy with the company’s goals, engaging stakeholders and advising leadership on critical decisions that drive long‑term value.  

Adoption rests on four pillars: 

  1. Clear communication: When you decide it’s time for your team to use AI-assisted solutions, explain why AI is being introduced and how it will change day‑to‑day work for practitioners.  
  2. Training: Show your team where the AI gets its data, how it works, and when to trust or override its recommendations. 
  3. Internal champions: Involve respected practitioners early on. Their endorsements carry more weight than top-down announcements.  
  4. Celebrate and learn: Acknowledge wins and share lessons from failures. Learning together builds trust and momentum.

Getting started: Pilot RFP automation with low-risk tests 

To ease into AI-based RFP automation, choose a low‑risk category like office supplies or basic maintenance, let the agent draft the RFP, and then review everything carefully. Use the process as a learning exercise to understand how the AI reasons and which prompts work best. 

You don’t need a perfectly mature function to start using AI. Basic building blocks like a supplier list (even a spreadsheet), past RFPs, templates, and an approval workflow are enough to get you started. Agentic AI can act like an experienced colleague on demand, surfacing best practices and standardized questions. Plus, it learns with every sourcing event through the feedback you give it. The more you use it and iterate with it, the smarter it becomes, which means future RFPs will require fewer clarifications and drive tighter pricing responses. 

Make it a shared experience by involving as many practitioners as you can. Listen to all of their feedback and make necessary adjustments. Over time this will help you refine your templates, scoring rubrics, and governance so the system learns your organization’s standards. 

Key takeaways 

  • Agentic AI needs the same briefing information you’d give a colleague, such as: objectives, scope, timeline, budget, evaluation criteria, and compliance constraints.
  • Historical artifacts (past RFPs, supplier performance, contracts) improve output.
  • The Sourcing Events Agent automates vendor research, standardizes RFPs tied to SAP data, and surfaces critical questions and risks.
  • Autonomy depends on risk tolerance; most teams use AI for discovery and drafting and keep humans for final judgments. 
  • Successful adoption requires communication, training, internal champions, and shared learning.

Want to learn the basic steps to implement AI in procurement?  

Read our new white paper with the Hackett Group for a practical roadmap and starter playbook. 

*Source: SAP Value Management

entry.title: Re: standard column: Units per Year
entry.content:
Normally bonus amounts are one-time pay components and not recurring and therefore do not have a frequency attached the same way that the recurring pay component used for base salary does.

Can you share more information about how your bonus planning is done? Is it a monthly recurring value? Can you provide a screenshot of the pay component being used for bonus?

Are you calculating bonuses on the salary worksheet and not using variable pay?

entry.title: CVE-2024-12797 vulnerability (OpenSSL) in Crystal Reports Runtime for VS
entry.content:
Various vulnerability scans show that Crystal Reports Runtime for VS is affected by several critical and high-level vulnerabilities.

The following files are reported:
libcrypto-3-x64.dll
libssl-3-x64.dll

The following vulnerabilities were found:
CVE-2024-12797
CVE-2025-15467
CVE-2025-9230

What actions are you taking to address and resolve these security issues? What is the timeline for this?

entry.title: Re: How to Take SAP’s New Performance-Based Certification Exams
entry.content:
HI @Biancadenreijer 
I was not able to find any SAP docs can provide that info about this but during your exam when you click on START you will be able see language options by following:

  1. Navigate to the Language menu at the bottom left of the video preview in your practice experience
  2. Select your preferred language from the dropdown list containing all 22 available options
  3. The language change will be applied to your assessment interface

The previous exam approach could cover:

  • Chinese
  • French
  • German
  • Japanese
  • Korean
  • Portuguese
  • Russian
  • Spanish

Hope this helps.
Thanks,

Amin

entry.title: Re: SAP Field Logistics: Next Generation Kitting & Staging for MRO Industries
entry.content:
Hi @MRO-Tim-McLain Field Logistics will work with both EWM and IM.  The deicision for EWM is about the management of the warehouse within the 4 walls and the business requirements.  Field Logistics can provide a path that offers the lean management that site operations want with the visability.  The key question here on the need of EWM is more about what is EWM trying to solve?  If the visability that is needed is about warehouse operations and functions then EWM is great fit but if the visability is about end to end supply chain then EWM will not solve this problem.

Cheers Simon

buyer has recreated rfq(s) a couple of times in a attempt to be able to clear the issue that is preventing them from printing/submitting rfq(s) to our suppliers. i have checked all the common areas of the part numbers, the requisition, supplier id and the rfq(s) and i ma not finding anything that would stop the buyer from printing/submitting the rfq(s) to the suppliers. what else can i check? is there a list of fixes for rfq related issues that can be provided to me? in my travels with this issue i did find an error message when i was in ME9A. would this have anything to do with our problem? Note, my sap privileges dont allow me to create/update purchasing products so if this is the problem, what do i need to tell my buyer so they can fix it?

No plant-specific conditions possible for item File open error:

Message No. ME398

Diagnosis

Item File open error: has been created for a specific plant. It is therefore not possible to enter conditions for different plants.

Procedure

If you wish to process the conditions for this item, go two steps back to the overview screen and choose the function from there.

entry.title: Na transação MD04
entry.content:
Na transação MD04 (MRP) atualmente o sistema SAP na KISS só possui 2 status no "MRP Elements" para o Pedido de compra:
1) POitem (Purchase Order Item): Represents an "open" purchase order.
2) "ShipNt": When a Purchase Order (PO) acknowledgement is posted with confirmation categories like "AB" (Order Confirmation) or "LA" (Shipping Notification), the MRP field in MD04 often displays "ShipNt" (Shipping Notification).

 

É solicitado configurar o "MRP Element" com 3 status ("Aberto"/"Confirmado"/"Embarcado") para o Pedido de Compra:
1) POitem (Purchase Order Item): Represents an "open" purchase order.
2) Acknw (Acknowledgment) Indicates a confirmation receipt from Vendor, purchase order with only an AB (Order Confirmation)
3) Shpgnt (Shipping Notification): represents material already delivered from Vendor with an LA (Shipping notification)
É possível realizar esta configuração no Standart

entry.title: Re: Max Number of settlement Rules
entry.content:
Dear @Paul_Richert,

What SAP says:

  • SAP delivers scenarios where the maximum number of distribution rules is set to 10
  • In Public Cloud you can maintain the settlement profile (incl. max number of distribution rules) via SSCUI 105768 Create Settlement Profile
  • Project settlement rules (WBS / work packages) use the settlement profile settings

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/c56f622a2edf491b9f1b596b55587009/6ca7d05275820226e10000000a4450e5.html

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3252133

What to do in Public Cloud:

1.In the app where you maintain the settlement rule for the work package/WBS, open Settlement Parameters and note the Settlement Profile

2.In CBC search and open SSCUI 105768 Create Settlement Profile and:

  • Open your settlement profile
  • Increase Max. number of distribution rules (set it to what you need)
  • Save

3.If the profile is not editable / New Entries is greyed out. That’s a known restriction for some delivered profiles or depending on how content is provided:

  • Some profiles may be greyed out / not editable in SSCUI 105768
  • Sometimes New Entries is greyed out

More details below:

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3690892

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3390229

4.Maintain the settlement rule again. After the profile change/assignment go back and add the additional receiver lines you should now be able to go beyond 10

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Building a Custom OTP-Based Authentication Page for SAP Commerce Composable Storefront using OAUTH2
entry.content:
In my previous blog   Custom Login Page  I have explained  how to create custom login using OUATH2 authentication 

This write-up reflects the exact OTP implementation : a two-step OTP experience (request code → verify code) that plugs into the existing custom login page flow, while also allowing both password login + OTP login on the same login page via CMS composition.

For OTP ( One time password login ) we need to do some configurations as mentioned in this help page link

Below is the sample impex to setup the OTP based  CMS components for your storefront

$contentCatalog=electronics-spaContentCatalog
$contentCV=catalogVersion(CatalogVersion.catalog(Catalog.id[default=$contentCatalog]),CatalogVersion.version[default=Online])[default=$contentCatalog:Online]


INSERT_UPDATE CMSFlexComponent;$contentCV[unique=true];uid[unique=true];name;flexType;&componentRef
;;ReturningCustomerOTPLoginComponent;Returning Customer OTP Login Component;ReturningCustomerOTPLoginComponent;ReturningCustomerOTPLoginComponent

INSERT_UPDATE CMSFlexComponent;$contentCV[unique=true];uid[unique=true];name;flexType;&componentRef
;;VerifyOTPTokenComponent;Verify One-time password token Component;VerifyOTPTokenComponent;VerifyOTPTokenComponent

INSERT_UPDATE ContentPage;$contentCV[unique=true];uid[unique=true];name;title;masterTemplate(uid,$contentCV);label;defaultPage[default='true'];approvalStatus(code)[default='approved'];homepage[default='false']
;;verify-otp-token;OTP token verification Page;Login;LoginPageTemplate;/login/verify-token

INSERT_UPDATE ContentSlot;$contentCV[unique=true];uid[unique=true];name;active;cmsComponents(&componentRef)
;;LeftContentSlot-verify-otp-token;Left Content Slot for OTP token verification;true;VerifyOTPTokenComponent

INSERT_UPDATE ContentSlotForPage;$contentCV[unique=true];uid[unique=true];position[unique=true];page(uid,$contentCV)[unique=true];contentSlot(uid,$contentCV)[unique=true]
;;LeftContent-verify-otp-token;LeftContentSlot;verify-otp-token;LeftContentSlot-verify-otp-token


UPDATE ContentSlot;$contentCV[unique=true];uid[unique=true];cmsComponents(uid, $contentCV)
;;LeftContentSlot-login;ReturningCustomerOTPLoginComponent,ReturningCustomerRegisterComponent;

Lets discuss what other changes are needed to get desired outcome

Architecture: 

Sequence Diagram 

1) What was added  as part of OTP login page (high level)

  • Password login stays as-is: a thin UI wrapper around Spartacus LoginFormComponentService.
  • OTP login adds two custom components:
    • Step 1: request verification token (triggers code delivery)
    • Step 2: verify code (posts to auth server login endpoint using tokenId/tokenCode)
  • CMS mapping wires SAP Commerce CMS components to our custom Angular components:
    • ReturningCustomerLoginComponent → custom password login UI
    • ReturningCustomerOTPLoginComponent → custom OTP request UI
    • VerifyOTPTokenComponent → custom OTP verify UI

2) Prerequisites (Commerce + Storefront)

SAP Commerce

  • Enable OTP on backend (property name depends on your Commerce version/config, commonly):
    • otp.customer.login.enabled=true
  • Ensure outbound delivery is configured (usually email).  The OTP code is delivered out-of-band email .You can use any fake SMTP server( MailHog, MailDev)  for local setup

Storefront (this repo already has it)

  • OAuth 2.0 auth server config points to the authorization server and custom login page endpoints. Example from spartacus-configuration.module.ts:
provideConfig(<AuthConfig>{
  authentication: {
    baseUrl: 'https://localhost:9002/authorizationserver',
    loginUrl: '/oauth/authorize',
    tokenEndpoint: '/oauth/token',
    customLoginPage: {
      csrfEndpoint: '/csrf',
      loginFormEndpoint: '/login'
    }
  }
})​
  • Feature toggle authorizationCodeFlowByDefault must be enabled (it is in your setup), because Spartacus’ custom login page flow uses CSRF + native form submission for auth-code.

3) Password login: “thin wrapper” pattern

The password login UI is intentionally simple: it binds the form and attributes (action, method, csrf) from LoginFormComponentService, then submits via service.login(nativeForm).

From custom-login.component.ts: 

export class CustomLoginComponent {
  @ViewChild('loginForm') loginForm?: ElementRef<HTMLFormElement>;

  form: UntypedFormGroup;
  isUpdating$: Observable<boolean>;
  csrf: CSRFResponse;
  action: string | undefined;
  method: string | undefined;

  constructor(private service: LoginFormComponentService) {
    this.form = this.service.form;
    this.isUpdating$ = this.service.isUpdating$;
    this.csrf = this.service.csrf;
    this.action = this.service.action;
    this.method = this.service.method;
    this.service.handleCustomLoginError();
  }

  onSubmit(): void {
    this.service.login(this.loginForm?.nativeElement);
  }
}

Why this matters: for OAuth2 custom login pages, it’s the service that ensures the CSRF and the form post behavior is correct (including session cookies and redirects).

4) OTP Step 1: request verification token (create tokenId + deliver code)

This step verifies the user’s base credentials and asks Commerce to create a verification token (and send the OTP code out-of-band).

From : custom-otp-login-componenet.ts

const ONE_TIME_PASSWORD_LOGIN_PURPOSE = 'LOGIN';

export class CustomOtpLoginComponent {
  private busy$ = new BehaviorSubject(false);

  isUpdating$: Observable<boolean> = this.busy$.pipe(
    tap((state) => {
      const userId = this.winRef.nativeWindow?.history?.state?.['newUid'];
      if (userId) {
        this.form.patchValue({ userId });
      }
      state === true ? this.form.disable() : this.form.enable();
    })
  );

  form = new UntypedFormGroup({
    userId: new UntypedFormControl('', [
      Validators.required,
      CustomFormValidators.emailValidator,
    ]),
    password: new UntypedFormControl('', Validators.required),
  });

  constructor(
    private routingService: RoutingService,
    private verificationTokenFacade: VerificationTokenFacade,
    private winRef: WindowRef
  ) {}

  onSubmit(): void {
    if (!this.form.valid) {
      this.form.markAllAsTouched();
      return;
    }

    this.busy$.next(true);

    const loginId = String(this.form.value.userId ?? '').toLowerCase();
    const password = String(this.form.value.password ?? '');

    this.verificationTokenFacade
      .createVerificationToken({
        loginId,
        password,
        purpose: ONE_TIME_PASSWORD_LOGIN_PURPOSE,
      })
      .subscribe({
        next: (result: any) => {
          this.routingService.go(
            { cxRoute: 'verifyToken' },
            {
              state: {
                loginId,
                password,
                tokenId: result?.tokenId,
                expiresIn: result?.expiresIn,
              },
            }
          );
        },
        error: (error: any) => {
          this.routingService.go(
            { cxRoute: 'verifyToken' },
            {
              state: {
                loginId,
                password,
                errorStatus: error?.status,
              },
            }
          );
          this.busy$.next(false);
        },
        complete: () => {
          this.form.reset();
          this.busy$.next(false);
        },
      });
  }
}

Key points:

  • We navigate to the verify page using cxRoute: 'verifyToken' and pass tokenId via history.state.
  • The OTP “code” is not returned to the UI. It is delivered by Commerce (email).

5) OTP Step 2: verify code via auth-server form POST

This is the most important integration detail: the auth server login expects a form POST.
To keep OAuth2 authorization code flow intact, we use Spartacus’ VerificationTokenFormComponentService, and we submit the native <form> similarly to the password login.

from custom-otp-verify-component.ts:

@Component({
  providers: [VerificationTokenFormComponentService],
})
export class CustomOtpVerifyComponent implements OnInit {
  @ViewChild('verificationTokenForm')
  verificationTokenForm?: ElementRef<HTMLFormElement>;

  form: UntypedFormGroup;
  isUpdating$: Observable<boolean>;

  csrf: CSRFResponse | undefined;
  action: string | undefined;
  method: string | undefined;

  constructor(
    private service: VerificationTokenFormComponentService,
    private routingService: RoutingService,
    private featureConfig: FeatureConfigService
  ) {
    this.form = this.service.form;
    this.isUpdating$ = this.service.isUpdating$;
    this.csrf = this.service.csrf;
    this.action = this.service.action;
    this.method = this.service.method;
  }

  ngOnInit(): void {
    const state = history.state ?? {};
    const tokenId = state['tokenId'] as string | undefined;
    const loginId = state['loginId'] as string | undefined;
    const password = state['password'] as string | undefined;

    history.pushState({ tokenId: '', password: '', loginId: '', errorStatus: '' }, 'verifyToken');

    if (!loginId || !password || !tokenId) {
      this.routingService.go(
        this.featureConfig.isEnabled('authorizationCodeFlowByDefault')
          ? { cxRoute: 'login' }
          : ['/login']
      );
      return;
    }

    this.form.patchValue({ tokenId });
  }

  onSubmit(): void {
    this.service.login(this.verificationTokenForm?.nativeElement);
  }
}

 

In the template, we intentionally post:

  • tokenId as username (hidden field)
  • tokenCode as password (user input)
  • CSRF hidden input with [name]="csrf.parameterName" so it matches auth-server expectations

From: custom-otp-verify-component.html

<input required="true" class="form-control" formControlName="tokenCode" name="password" />

<input type="hidden" formControlName="tokenId" name="username" />

<ng-container *cxFeature="'authorizationCodeFlowByDefault'">
  <input *ngIf="csrf" formControlName="csrf" type="hidden" [name]="csrf.parameterName" />
</ng-container>

6) CMS component mapping (the wiring that makes it work in SmartEdit)

All three CMS components are mapped to custom Angular components in:  custom-login-module.ts

provideConfig(<CmsConfig>{
  cmsComponents: {
    ReturningCustomerLoginComponent: {
      component: CustomLoginComponent,
      guards: [NotAuthGuard, CustomLoginGuard],
      providers: [
        {
          provide: LoginFormComponentService,
          useClass: LoginFormComponentService,
          deps: [AuthService, GlobalMessageService, WindowRef]
        }
      ]
    },
    ReturningCustomerOTPLoginComponent: {
      component: CustomOtpLoginComponent,
      guards: [NotAuthGuard, CustomLoginGuard],
    },
    VerifyOTPTokenComponent: {
      component: CustomOtpVerifyComponent,
      guards: [NotAuthGuard, CustomLoginGuard],
    }
  }
})

Why the guards matter:

  • CustomLoginGuard ensures these components render in the correct “custom login page” context (during the OAuth authorize flow)

7) Where does the verification code come from?

  • The code is delivered by SAP Commerce via configured channel (typically email).
When the login is successful it will go back to homepage 

 

 

The Token Verification Screen 

Happy to share implementation tips or a troubleshooting checklist (CSRF/session cookie mismatches, redirect loops, email delivery in lower environments) if you’re working through a similar integration.

Code described above are can be found in this OTPLogin Code

entry.title: Re: SAP Cloud Transport Management on BTP
entry.content:
Dear @Vikas4,

In Public Cloud the content you can move via the CTMS-based transport process (i.e., handled in practice through Export Customizing Transports / Export Software Collection and imported via Import Collection) falls into these buckets:

1.Business Configuration (Customizing) transports

These are the configuration changes recorded in customizing requests and exported with Export Customizing Transports

CBC transports must be handled/imported in the correct sequence (dependency chain)

More details below:

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3414407

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/a630d57fc5004c6383e7a81efee7a8bb/a772a0f9fba3427aa0a2036bc07c24ec.html

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3547695

https://me.sap.com/notes/0003448385

https://learning.sap.com/courses/administrating-sap-s-4hana-cloud-public-edition/describing-types-of-transport-requests_a62ec46a-cc56-4bb6-8124-89bda8bbaab4

2.Key-User extensibility + IAM/UX content via Software Collections

These are transported using Export Software Collection imported via Import Collection

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/0f69f8fb28ac4bf48d2b57b9637e81fa/6896de5f70c54753ac028f444eafa16f.html

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/0f69f8fb28ac4bf48d2b57b9637e81fa/74940689b4a54c89a04f11dc2247701a.html

Examples of items commonly transported in Software Collections:

  • Custom Fields, Custom Logic, Custom CDS Views, Custom Analytical Queries, Custom Business Objects, custom code lists/libraries SAP lists these as supported extension items
  • UI adaptations / UI5 flexibility changes tracked as software collection items
  • Business roles, business catalogs, launchpad spaces and pages authorization/launchpad content is moved via software collections
  • Email templates maintained in Maintain Email Templates are intended to be created in the right system and transported via Export Software Collection
  • Output forms / texts / logos can also be moved via Export/Import Software Collection in the Output Management context

More details below:

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/2914822

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3168405

https://learning.sap.com/courses/managing-user-identity-and-access-in-sap-s-4hana-cloud-public-edition/exporting-software-collections_fa08b98a-7725-4ed1-a4bc-ec8a8f9f4e34

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3535312

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/2745326

3.Developer extensibility

ABAP/developer extensibility artifacts are also imported using Import Collection in the target tenants

More details below:

https://learning.sap.com/courses/administrating-sap-s-4hana-cloud-public-edition/processing-transports-in-sap-s-4hana-cloud-public-edition_cb8abf97-aab1-4c3f-b5dc-b12bd8b2ad67

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3482742

Summary:

CTMS transports are for configuration and development artifacts, not for moving master data or transactional data those are handled via migration/replication tools, not transports

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Re: Consume CDS View inside CDS Table Function by using AMDP
entry.content:
This view i am using in the Table function

--------------------------------------------------

@AbapCatalog.viewEnhancementCategory: [#NONE]

@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #CHECK

@EndUserText.label: 'Demo CDS View'

@Metadata.ignorePropagatedAnnotations: true

@ObjectModel.usageType: {

serviceQuality: #X,

sizeCategory: #S,

dataClass: #MIXED

}

define view entity ZI_FLIGHT_DEMO_CDS

as select from /dmo/flight

{

key carrier_id as CarrierId,

key connection_id as ConnectionId,

key flight_date as FlightDate,

@Semantics.amount.currencyCode: 'CurrencyCode'

price as Price,

currency_code as CurrencyCode,

plane_type_id as PlaneTypeId,

seats_max as SeatsMax,

seats_occupied as SeatsOccupied

}

---------------------------------------------------------

This is my Table Function

---------------------------------------------------------

@EndUserText.label: 'Demo CDS Table function'

@ClientHandling.type: #CLIENT_DEPENDENT

define table function ZI_FLIGHT_DEMO_TF

with parameters

@Environment.systemField: #CLIENT

p_date: abap.clnt

returns

{

key client : abap.clnt ;

key carrier_id : /dmo/carrier_id ;

key connection_id : /dmo/connection_id ;

key flight_date : /dmo/flight_date ;

currency_code : /dmo/currency_code;

seats_max : /dmo/plane_seats_max;

seats_occupied : /dmo/plane_seats_occupied;

 

}

implemented by method

zcl_101_tf=>GETDATA;

------------------------------------------------------------

This is my class

------------------------------------------------------------

CLASS zcl_101_tf DEFINITION

PUBLIC

FINAL

CREATE PUBLIC .

 

PUBLIC SECTION.

INTERFACES : if_amdp_marker_hdb.

CLASS-METHODS : getData FOR TABLE FUNCTION zi_flight_demo_tf.

PROTECTED SECTION.

PRIVATE SECTION.

ENDCLASS.

 

 

CLASS zcl_101_tf IMPLEMENTATION.

METHOD getData BY DATABASE FUNCTION FOR HDB LANGUAGE SQLSCRIPT

OPTIONS READ-ONLY USING zi_flight_demo_cds.

 

RETURN seleCT _raw.mandt as client,

_raw.carrierid as CarrierId,

_raw.connectionid as ConnectionId,

_raw.flightdate as FlightDate,

_raw.currencycode as CurrencyCode,

_raw.planetypeid as PlaneTypeId,

_raw.seatsmax as SeatsMax,

_raw.seatsoccupied as SeatsOccupied

from zi_flight_demo_cds as _raw

WHERE _raw.mandt = :p_clnt;

 

endMETHOD.

 

ENDCLASS.

 

I am getting this error : The method "GETDATA" implements the CDS table function "ZI_FLIGHT_DEMO_TF". The client handling annotation for "ZI_FLIGHT_DEMO_TF" is incomplete or wrong in the current language version.

entry.title: Na transação MD04 (MRP) atualmente o sistema SAP só possui 2 status no "MRP Elements"
entry.content:
Na transação MD04 (MRP) atualmente o sistema SAP na KISS só possui 2 status no "MRP Elements" para o Pedido de compra:
1) POitem (Purchase Order Item): Represents an "open" purchase order.
2) "ShipNt": When a Purchase Order (PO) acknowledgement is posted with confirmation categories like "AB" (Order Confirmation) or "LA" (Shipping Notification), the MRP field in MD04 often displays "ShipNt" (Shipping Notification).

 

É possível configurar no Standart o "MRP Element" com 3 status ("Aberto"/"Confirmado"/"Embarcado") para o Pedido de Compra:AN
1) POitem (Purchase Order Item): Represents an "open" purchase order.
2) Acknw (Acknowledgment) Indicates a confirmation receipt from Vendor, purchase order with only an AB (Order Confirmation)
3) Shpgnt (Shipping Notification): represents material already delivered from Vendor with an LA (Shipping notification)

entry.title: Re: No Access to SAP Cloud CBC Demo
entry.content:
Dear @Rudi-Amanuel-Sadik,

In Public Cloud access to SAP Central Business Configuration (CBC) depends on the standard CBC user setup and Identity Provisioning (IPS) provisioning flow. If users are created in IAS / S/4 tenant but not provisioned to CBC via IPS opening the CBC URL typically ends with Unauthorized

More details below:

https://me.sap.com/notes/0003236033

https://me.sap.com/notes/0003103503

Your screenshot shows the IPS bundle tenant was created in 2016. SAP explains that older IPS bundle tenants delivered before March 15, 2022 run on SAP BTP, Neo environment and are handled differently than newer tenants on the SAP Cloud Identity Services infrastructure. In these older setups the IPS administration is often not exposed in the IAS UI in the same way, also SAP has a known symptom where IAS admins don’t see IPS-related groups/apps/settings in IAS even though IPS exists it’s documented as a Note scenario

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/cloud-identity-services/cloud-identity-services/tenant-model-and-licensing

https://me.sap.com/notes/0003341095

What to do:

A: Try to access and use IPS directly (not via the IAS menu)

Follow the CBC User Setup and Access procedure:

  • In IPS go to Source Systems and open the enabled CBC source system
  • Go to Jobs and run the required provisioning jobs at minimum the read job to pull required structures and the user provisioning jobs to push users/groups as required

SAP also provides the IPS configuration guidance for CBC connections

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/doc/044aad0485094708ab5bb068bf77c997/SHIP3.0/en-US/SAPCentralBusinessConfiguration_UserSetupandAccess_en-US.pdf

https://help.sap.com/docs/identity-provisioning/identity-provisioning/target-sap-central-business-configuration

https://help.sap.com/docs/identity-provisioning/identity-provisioning/proxy-sap-central-business-configuration

B: Validate that CBC is visible in IPS as a Source/Target system

If CBC is not listed in IPS Source/Target systems even though the demo was delivered this is a known issue pattern and typically needs SAP to fix the provisioning setup/bundling

More details below:

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3147144

C: If IPS is present but access still fails follow SAP CBC access troubleshooting

SAP provides a checklist specifically for CBC access issues with Unauthorized focused on verifying that IPS is set up correctly

More details below:

https://me.sap.com/notes/0003103503

Do you need a new tenant from SAP?

Possibly, YES if:

  • you cannot access the IPS admin console at all, or
  • CBC is not visible in IPS Source/Target systems, or
  • the old Neo-based IPS bundle tenant prevents the required provisioning setup

In that case xthe practical resolution is to log an SAP incident and request that SAP correct the CBC<->IPS bundling so CBC appears in IPS or provide a properly configured IPS tenant ideally on SAP Cloud Identity Services infrastructure for your demo landscape

More details below:

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3147144

https://help.sap.com/docs/cloud-identity-services/cloud-identity-services/tenant-model-and-licensing

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Re: Is it possible to query dimension and measure metadat...
entry.content:
No problem - now I see the issue.

The full answer to your question is actually rather long one, and if you're new to SAP and Datasphere, I'll spare you the long answer (let me know if want that instead).

The much shorter answer to your question is:

  1. Technically, yes, you could query the SYS tables, but they only contain the technical names. However
    1. You'll have to combine TABLE_COLUMNS and the VIEW_COLUMNS tables to be able to query the full set of objects.
      They contain all the columns for all the tables and views. 
    2. This approach does not take object dependency into consideration. A view may be based on a view, that is based on several several other views, that might all be the same data, just in different contexts.
    3. Since a view can be based on more views, you might get a several views that all contain the "AmountLocalCurrency" field - and they may, or may not, be related. 
    4. The SYS tables are pretty well hidden, and you have to be an administrator, and jump through several hoops to expose them for external consumption. 
      Even if you can garantee that you can find exactly what you're looking for, it 's going to be a massive pain in the... nevermind. 

  2. If the SYS tables only store the technical names, where are the business named stored, you might reasonably ask?
    1. Nowhere. They are stored nowhere you can reach them. 
    2. You may have noticed, that on all Datasphere objects, you have the option to "Export CSN / JSON"

      CSN, or Core Schema Notation, is a custom format build by SAP, for SAP. 

      The file will actually contain everything you need for a given view. 
      In the example below "ControllingArea" is the technical name, and "CO-område" is the business name:

      You can get these files, if you're brave enough the use the Command-Line API, and then work through them to find all the measures. You''ll have to loop through all the views, unless you know what you are looking for. 
      These files are very long and painfully talkative, because each object file contains the entire data model for that object. All the views, tables, calculations, ect. that feed into a single view, so if you can decode the bloody thing, it'll be exactly what you need. 

In conclusion: No, Datasphere has no solution you can query and get what you require - it has to be built, either by you (and a couple AIs) or by whatever team is managing your Datasphere solution. And they won't have built this - very few people have, because it is a colossal pain in the ass - but they might have some technical documentation that will give your some info. 

Sorry I can't be more helpful 🙂

Did you know you can check if your data model can access the relevant data without using an access control. In this case, I have a CDS view entity which has access control defined. Let’s check the data preview and it's limited to two records. Now click on SQL Console. To get the select statement and addition of with privileged access disabled, the access control.

00;04;11;16 - 00;04;34;26
Shilpa Shankar
Your user needs specific permission to execute ABAP source code which contains the with privileged access addition. Now this enables you to check which data a CDS entity results with or without CDS access control. In case if data is missing or only part of the expected data is displayed in the data preview, then you might not have the appropriate permissions to access the relevant data.

entry.title: BTC - (Business Transformation Cycle) step sequence unclear
entry.content:
When migrating from ECC classic GL/classic AA to S/4 HANA, after the system is technically upgraded to S/4 HANA (SUM) but with no data, there are some steps to execute.

  1. There are some customizing steps to execute to support the data migration into S/4 HANA new tables/structures (Example: define the currency settings for migration and conversion logic, define Doc Type mapping CO Business Transaction, assign variants for Mapping CO Business transactions, ledger and accounting principle assignment, etc.) . All these steps can be found in the SPRO Menu: SPRO>Conversion of Accounting to S/4 HANA > Preparations and Migration of Customizing .
  2. After the customizing steps are done, there are some data migration steps to execute (Regenerating CDS Views and fields mapping, Transactional Data analysis and then migration of the data). These steps can be found in the SPRO Menu: SPRO>Conversion of Accounting to S/4 HANA > Data Migration.

Would like to get clarification about the sequence of these steps above within the BTC cycle steps. We are currently at the BTC Cycle execution step. Next step is the BTC Post-processing. Would like to know:

  1. If both Customizing steps (1) and data migration steps (2) should be done just before the BTC Post-Processing step?
  2. Or if Customizing steps (1) should be done before BTC Post-Processing step and Data migration steps (2) after BTC Post-Processing step?
  3. Or if both Customizing steps (1) and data migration steps (2) should be done after BTC Post-Processing step?
  4. Or if Customizing steps (1) should be done before BTC Post-Processing step and BTC Post-Processing handles the Data migration?

entry.title: P Card Receiving
entry.content:
Some users in our institute have P cards, and they are allowed to buy materials for direct projects and we would like to track that spend and match it to the project. There are 2 problems:

1) the P Card charge only shows a total amount, and not the quantity ordered, so we have no way to receive against the quantity.

2) We are a government contractor, and on government projects we need to track the actual receipt date. We have to reconcile all items to pay the bill, but if the item is not received yet, the receipt defaults to the transaction date.

What is the best approach to address these issues?

entry.title: Re: Clarification on Automatic Payment Block After Reset...
entry.content:
Dear @rohini_dutta21,

The Reset Cleared Items app (F2223) is documented to reset the clearing of line items and optionally reverse the clearing entry. That documented scope does not include any step to automatically set a payment block on the invoice that becomes open again

More details below:

https://fioriappslibrary.hana.ondemand.com/sap/fix/externalViewer/#/detail/Apps('F2223')/S35

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/2682947

Payment blocking is handled via separate standard functions/apps, for example:

  • Manage Payment Blocks V2 (F0593A) set/remove payment blocks on invoices or supplier accounts
  • Manage Supplier Line Items (F0712) includes the ability to Block for Payment for supplier open items

More details below:

https://fioriappslibrary.hana.ondemand.com/sap/fix/externalViewer/#/detail/Apps('F0712')/S35

https://fioriappslibrary.hana.ondemand.com/sap/fix/externalViewer/#/detail/Apps('F0593A')/S35

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3269289

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3524950

So there is no standard configuration that ties Reverse+Reset in F2223 to auto payment block on the reopened invoice

This can be achieved but typically via extensibility, not a dedicated Reset Cleared Items standard alert

Option A: Situation Handling (Extended Framework) in Public Cloud

You can create your own situation types using the extended framework (object-based or message-based) and send notifications to responsible users/teams. SAP also provides a list of delivered Finance situation templates but Reset Cleared Items executed is not a commonly delivered standard template you’d model a custom one if needed

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/6b39bd1d0e5e4099a5b65d835c29c696/a35272ba712440e4811395d4ffe83741.html

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/a630d57fc5004c6383e7a81efee7a8bb/92a58a164a4c4320bd6bf563d745baca.html

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/a630d57fc5004c6383e7a81efee7a8bb/60748ea9f37d4692912578b698aaefee.html

Option B: Event-driven alert

A reversal/reset results in accounting changes and can be picked up via business events for example, Journal Entry Event.

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/b978f98fc5884ff2aeb10c8fdeb8a43b/a97fa43157424cf582cd49baf9b708c1.html

Summary:

  • Standard config: Auto-block reopened invoice after Reset Cleared Items is not supported (payment block is a separate process/app)
  • Extensibility: Near-real-time notification is feasible via Situation Handling Extended or event-driven automation
  • Auto-blocking via extension: possible only if there is a released and supported method (API/automation step) to update the payment block for the reopened open item in your Public Cloud scope

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: SAP Databricks - Data Classification : A Practical Guide for Modern Data Governance
entry.content:
As organizations move more of their SAP and enterprise workloads into modern lakehouse platforms, data governance becomes a critical priority. Sensitive data—such as personal identifiers, financial details, and confidential business information—must be identified quickly and protected consistently.

This is where Data Classification in SAP Databricks plays a vital role, especially when working with SAP data products shared between Datasphere via BDC or pipelines on the Databricks Lakehouse.

In this blog, we’ll explore what data classification means, why it matters, and how Databricks simplifies classification for SAP‑related datasets. 

What Is Data Classification?

Data Classification is the process of automatically identifying and labeling sensitive information stored across your data environment.

SAP Databricks provides an intelligent scanner that examines:

  • Table metadata
  • Column names
  • Column content patterns
  • Statistical characteristics

Based on this analysis, Databricks applies tags such as:

  • PII (Personally Identifiable Information)
  • Financial Data
  • Confidential
  • Internal Only

These tags enable automated governance, access controls, lineage tracking, and risk assessments—all essential for SAP data that often contains personal or financial details.

How Databricks Enables Data Classification

Below screenshot shows the feature inside SAP Databricks Catalog Explorer → Details → Advanced for a catalog named “<mycatalog>”.

Under the Advanced section, you can see:

Data Classification: Disabled

With an option to Enable. Once enabled, Databricks will:

  1. Scan all tables in the selected catalog
  2. Detect sensitive columns (e.g., customer numbers, emails, IBANs, tax IDs)
  3. Apply classification tags automatically
  4. Make tags visible to administrators, governance teams, and data stewards
  5. Feed the metadata into Unity Catalog governance policies

This is particularly powerful for SAP workloads because:

  • SAP systems generate complex, interconnected tables
  • Sensitive data is often embedded deep in transactional structures
  • Manual tracking is nearly impossible at scale

Why Data Classification Matters ?

When SAP Delta sharing (Zero copy) lands in Databricks—whether through SAP Datasphere or custom ETL flows—it frequently includes regulated data fields.

Data classification supports:

Compliance (GDPR, HIPAA, SOX, etc.)

Automatically tag and monitor sensitive data.

Least‑privilege access

Policies can enforce who can view what.

Secure analytics

Sensitive data is masked or tokenized before analytics or ML workloads access it.

Automated governance workflows

Classification integrates with Unity Catalog, allowing:

  • Row/column-level security
  • Access auditing
  • Change management
  • Data lineage tracking

How to Enable Data Classification in Databricks (Step-by-Step)

  1. Open Catalog Explorer in Databricks
  2. Select the target catalog (e.g., skf)
  3. Open the Details tab
  4. Scroll to the Advanced section
  5. Toggle Data Classification → Enable
  6. Databricks begins automatic scanning in the background
  7. Review classifications under Table → Column Details

After enabling, every new table ingested into the catalog will be scanned automatically.

What Happens After Classification?

Once tags are generated, you can:

  • View sensitive columns
    • Under each table’s schema view.
  • Create governance rules
    • Using Unity Catalog’s policy engine (e.g., hide PII unless user is in allowed group).
  • Implement data masking
    • Auto mask email, phone, or ID fields for non‑privileged users.
  • Monitor sensitive data flows
    • Using lineage dashboards.

Real‑World Use Case: SAP Customer Data Migration

Imagine for ML Usecase or Analytics, Delta Sharing from Datasphere of SAP Sales & Distribution or SAP CRM or S/4 HANA customer tables into SAP Databricks. These contain:

  • Customer Names
  • Addresses
  • Contact Info
  • Tax IDs
  • Payment Terms

By enabling Data Classification:

  • Databricks identifies PII automatically
  • Data engineers do not manually inspect thousands of customer fields
  • Security teams gain full visibility
  • Access policies enforce compliance from day one

Conclusion

Data Classification in Databricks is a key enabler for secure, compliant, and scalable SAP analytics.
With just one click, you activate an automated governance engine that keeps your SAP datasets protected, discoverable, and compliant.

entry.title: ZUGFERD vs XRECHNUNG-B2B Customer Invoices from External System
entry.content:
Hi,

In Germany, we can exchange B2B invoices with customers in two ways.

1. via PEPPOL Network (supported via XRECHNUNG profile supported via UBL 2.1)

2. via Email (supported via ZUGFERD supported by CII)

We may have customers who wants invoice in PDF embedded with XML and other may want Invoice to be send over Peppol as pure XML XRechnung invoice. For our client 99 percent of customer invoices are generated in a non-SAP systems.

Now for a customer who opts for Peppol exchange and an invoice is created in external system and the middleware transform it to UBL 2.1 format invoice to be consumed in SAP DRC and SAP DRC creates an eDocument for it so that it can be send over Peppol to the customer, this invoice exchange happens using pure XML Rechnung profile supported by UBL 2.1 format.

Question: For a customer who wishes to have invoice via email method ,PDF (embedded with XML) ZUGFERD profile , will the middleware have to transform the invoice from non-SAP system to CII format rather than UBL 2.1 and then send it to SAP DRC to create an eDocument and then send it to the customer via eDoc_cockpit OR SAP DRC will use UBL 2.1 format to generate an XML which will be consumed by SAP DRC and then SAP convert into a format which is compatible with ZUGFERD (CII) so that it can be send over to customer via Email.

entry.title: AVC Procedure Dependency Error When Referencing Variant Table in CU01
entry.content:
Hello SAP experts,

We would like to manage price variants based on intervals defined in a characteristic. For this purpose, we created an AVC variant table in CU60 with two characteristics: ZLARGEUR_NOMINAL and Y_TEST_PROB_CARACT.

However, when we create an AVC procedure dependency in CU01 using the following editor logic:

 
table Z_PRICE_INTERVAL
( ZLARGEUR_NOMINAL = ZLARGEUR_NOMINAL,
Y_TEST_PROB_CARACT = $self.Y_TEST_PROB_CARACT )

the system displays the following error message:

"Unable to process error message."

Could you please advise what might be causing this issue and how to resolve it?

Thank you in advance for your support.

entry.title: Re: Clean Core – Part 1 – Activation of RISE with SAP Methodology Dashboard on Cloud ALM
entry.content:
Hi Manuel,
You are right, as SCMON and SUSG are run in prod system, it does make sense to execute ATC check in PRD system. At present the values from SCMON & SUSG are Execution of Custom objects (percentage & 3-month) and Unused customer objects (percentage and actual numbers).

If you want to check clean core levels of development that hasn’t yet been moved into Prod, you can do the ATC check in Development/Test. However in this case execution & usage figures do not provide useful information.

entry.title: Result Analysis - Final Settlement - TECO Phase
entry.content:
Greetings everyone,

I'm working on 3 different Result Analysis Methods (Rev Base and Cost Based POC and Complete Contract Method) and currently until the cancellation phase and even with the cancellation of all RA postings I'm fine with all of them. I'm just having an issue or maybe a doubt around my expectation with sending the primary account balances at the WBS Element level which are the GL accounts that has been defined in the OKG5. I'm not able to settle these values like in a normal non RA Related project scenario at the end of the project which I was expecting it to be that way once I get a RA WBS to TECO. I would expect reversing every RA posting which is happening this is fine but the actual values like COGS from delivery or a Manuel Customer Invoice Entry's GL accounts that are defined in the OKG5 just sits there at the end of project (For Complete Contract it is sending them with internal settlement Secondary accounts which I have linked with OKG4 GLs in the okao but it is still doesn't really makes sense to me since it is still settling with secondary cost elements even project is finished). I would expect the primary ones being settled with primary ones at the end of the project once we take the WBS to TECO to wherever we would like to settle just like a normal non-RA project scenario. In the Cost based and Revenue Based it is not even settling anything once WBS is tech. It is just reversing everything.

I just wanted to ask from your experiences if RA designed this way or am I missing some config?


Thank you so much and best wishes.

entry.title: BiWhy – BOBJ Cleanup: Reducing BIAR Export from 1.5 TB to 9 GB
entry.content:
Disclaimer:
BiWhy is available again for FREE to everyone on biwhy.net

The most important step before migrating a SAP BusinessObjects system is cleanup. It can reduce repository size by orders of magnitude and make migrations significantly faster and safer.

Here is one real case example.

Quick win

 BeforeAfter
CMS objects232k50k
Repository size503 GB11 GB
BIAR export estimate~1.5 TB9 GB
Export timeimpractical75 sec

The Problem

During migration preparation, a full system export was attempted using Promotion Management.

The wizard estimated the export size at ~1.5 TB.

Multi-phase promotion is possible, but for small and medium systems a Full Content Promotion is usually simpler and more reliable.

Retention limits are not always helpful because:

  • limits trigger only after the next scheduled run
  • some reports no longer have active schedules
  • limits do not apply to files copied into user folders

Initial System State

CMS Repository

  • CMS objects: 232k
  • Files: 184k
  • Size: 503 GB

TXT / Excel Instances

  • Objects: 34k
  • Files: 31k
  • Size: 479 GB

Platform Search Delta Index

  • Files: 149k
  • Size: 14 GB

Solution

Instances older than one week were removed using BiWhy.

The process is simple.

1. Load instances

Run a CMS query, for example:

SELECT SI_ID, SI_NAME
FROM CI_INFOOBJECTS
WHERE SI_INSTANCE = 1
AND SI_KIND IN ('Txt','Excel')
AND SI_UPDATE_TS < '2026.03.01'

2. Review results

Objects returned by the query can be inspected directly in the repository tree.

3. Delete instances

BiWhy allows deletion:

  • individually
  • recursively inside selected folders
  • all selected objects at once

NB: Because BiWhy allows deletion of general object types, always test the procedure in a non-production environment first.

During testing, use SELECT * to inspect all properties of a sample object you plan to delete.

Important Detail – Recurring Instances

Recurring objects:

  • have modified timestamp = initial creation time
  • use the same SI_KIND as the delivery format

For example, we do not expect Excel (SI_KIND='Excel') to represent a schedule – but it can.

To avoid deleting them, apply additional filters:

SI_RECURRING
SI_SCHEDULE_STATUS

SI_SCHEDULE_STATUS values:

  • COMPLETE = 1
  • EXPIRED = 13
  • FAILURE = 3
  • PAUSED = 8
  • PENDING = 9
  • RUNNING = 0
  • WARNING = 14

Platform Search Cleanup

Clean up Platform Search delta index files by performing a search index rebuild.

Before cleanup

After cleanup

Context delete action
